papers.citation_key: Neuhaus EM, Zhang W, Gelis L, Deng Y, Noldus J, Hatt H (2009

ground_truth.citation: Neuhaus EM, Zhang W, Gelis L, Deng Y, Noldus J, Hatt H (2009). "Activation of an olfactory receptor inhibits proliferation of prostate cancer cells." J Biol Chem 284(24):16218-16225.
ground_truth.claim: Activation of OR51E2 (PSGR) by beta-ionone inhibits proliferation of prostate cancer cells.
ground_truth.mechanism: Beta-ionone binding triggers intracellular Ca2+ increase and MAPK family activation (p44/42, p38, SAPK/JNK).
ground_truth.model_system: LNCaP cells (endogenous receptor); also HEK293, PC-3, primary prostate epithelial cells
ground_truth.verification_notes: Carried over from original 5-paper benchmark; endpoint field added (was previously implicit only).

llm_extraction.mechanism: MAPK family activation (p44/42, p38, SAPK/JNK) and intracellular Ca2+ increase
llm_extraction.model_system: LNCaP cells (endogenous PSGR), HEK293 (heterologous), primary prostate epithelial cells
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Full text confirms beta-ionone-induced Ca2+ and MAPK activation inhibits LNCaP proliferation; ERK1/2 specifically noted as NOT modulated, only p38/SAPK-JNK, MAPK family label overall accurate.

papers.citation_key: Sanz G, Leray I, Dewaele A, Sobilo J, Lerondel S, Bouet S, G

ground_truth.citation: Sanz G, Leray I, Dewaele A, Sobilo J, Lerondel S, Bouet S, Grebert D, Monnerie R, Pajot-Augy E, Mir LM (2014). "Promotion of cancer cell invasiveness and metastasis emergence caused by olfactory receptor stimulation." PLoS ONE 9(1):e85110.
ground_truth.claim: Beta-ionone stimulation of PSGR/OR51E2 promotes LNCaP cell invasiveness; alpha-ionone (antagonist) blocks the effect.
ground_truth.mechanism: PI3K-gamma / Gbeta-gamma G-protein pathway (collagen-gel invasion assay); NSG xenograft metastasis assay in vivo.
ground_truth.model_system: LNCaP cells; NSG mouse xenograft
ground_truth.verification_notes: Endpoint is invasiveness, NOT proliferation -- the key case distinguishing this from Neuhaus 2009 (different endpoint, not necessarily a same-endpoint contradiction).

llm_extraction.mechanism: PI3K-gamma / Gbeta-gamma G-protein pathway
llm_extraction.model_system: LNCaP cells (collagen gel invasion assay); NSG mouse xenograft (in vivo metastasis)
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Full text distinguishes this from proliferation assays -- invasion index via collagen gel, plus in vivo metastasis in NSG mice. Alpha-ionone blocks the pro-invasive effect.

papers.citation_key: Rodriguez M, Luo W, Weng J, Zeng L, Yi Z, Siwko S, Liu M (20

ground_truth.citation: Rodriguez M, Luo W, Weng J, Zeng L, Yi Z, Siwko S, Liu M (2014). "PSGR promotes prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia and prostate cancer xenograft growth through NF-kappaB." Oncogenesis 3:e114.
ground_truth.claim: PSGR overexpression drives chronic inflammation, PIN, and larger xenograft tumors via NF-kB.
ground_truth.mechanism: NF-kB pathway activation downstream of PSGR overexpression in a transgenic probasin-driven mouse model.
ground_truth.model_system: PSGR transgenic mouse; LNCaP xenograft
ground_truth.verification_notes: Carried over from original 5-paper benchmark.

llm_extraction.endpoint: prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia and xenograft tumor growth
llm_extraction.mechanism: NF-kB pathway
llm_extraction.model_system: PSGR transgenic mouse (probasin-driven); LNCaP xenograft
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: PSGR overexpression drives chronic inflammation leading to PIN, and increases xenograft tumor size, via NF-kB.

papers.citation_key: Pronin A, Slepak V (2021). "Ectopically expressed olfactory

ground_truth.citation: Pronin A, Slepak V (2021). "Ectopically expressed olfactory receptors OR51E1 and OR51E2 suppress proliferation and promote cell death in a prostate cancer cell line." J Biol Chem 296:100475.
ground_truth.claim: Inducible OR51E1/OR51E2 expression in LNCaP suppresses proliferation and promotes cell death; explicitly flags field-wide controversy over whether beta-ionone is a real OR51E2 agonist.
ground_truth.mechanism: Adenylyl cyclase/cAMP signaling upon short/medium-chain fatty acid stimulation (not beta-ionone, which the authors could not confirm as an agonist in their hands).
ground_truth.model_system: LNCaP cells (inducible expression)
ground_truth.verification_notes: Carried over from original 5-paper benchmark.

llm_extraction.mechanism: adenylyl cyclase / cAMP signaling
llm_extraction.model_system: LNCaP cells (inducible OR51E1/OR51E2 expression)
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Paper explicitly could not confirm beta-ionone as an OR51E2 agonist in their assay system and flags this as an open field-wide controversy -- important caveat beyond the plain direction/mechanism fields.

papers.citation_key: Gelis L, Jovancevic N, Veitinger S, Mandal B, Arndt HD, Neuh

ground_truth.citation: Gelis L, Jovancevic N, Veitinger S, Mandal B, Arndt HD, Neuhaus EM, Hatt H (2017). "Functional expression of olfactory receptors in human primary melanoma and melanoma metastasis." Exp Dermatol 26(7):569-576.
ground_truth.claim: OR51E2 is upregulated in melanoma vs normal melanocytes; beta-ionone activation inhibits VGP melanoma cell proliferation via Ca2+ signaling.
ground_truth.mechanism: Beta-ionone-induced intracellular Ca2+ increase, RNAi-confirmed OR51E2-dependence.
ground_truth.model_system: Human primary melanoma tissue; VGP melanoma cells
ground_truth.verification_notes: Carried over from original 5-paper benchmark.

llm_extraction.mechanism: intracellular Ca2+ signaling
llm_extraction.model_system: human primary melanoma tissue; VGP melanoma cells
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Abstract only (no PMC full text available). OR51E2 upregulated in melanoma vs normal melanocytes; beta-ionone activation inhibits VGP melanoma cell growth via Ca2+, confirmed by RNAi.

papers.citation_key: Marelli G, et al. (2025). "Chemosensor receptors are lipid-d

ground_truth.citation: Marelli G, et al. (2025). "Chemosensor receptors are lipid-detecting regulators of macrophage function in cancer."
ground_truth.claim: OR51E2 on tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) binds palmitic acid, driving a tumor-supportive macrophage phenotype in prostate cancer; TAM-specific chemosensor deletion causes cancer regression.
ground_truth.mechanism: Palmitic acid - OR51E2 binding on TAMs (not tumor cells); CRISPR screen + in vivo TAM-specific knockout.
ground_truth.model_system: Primary macrophage CRISPR screen; TAM-specific in vivo knockout; human prostate cancer tissue (spatial lipidomics)
ground_truth.verification_notes: Cell-non-autonomous mechanism -- the receptor acts in TAMs, not tumor cells. Deliberately included to test whether extraction conflates 'OR51E2 direction' with tumor-cell-intrinsic direction when the actual locus of action is stromal/immune.

llm_extraction.mechanism: palmitic acid binding to OR51E2 on macrophages (CRISPR screen)
llm_extraction.model_system: primary macrophage genome-wide CRISPR screen; in vivo TAM-specific chemosensor knockout; human prostate cancer tissue spatial lipidomics
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Receptor acts in tumor-associated macrophages, not tumor cells themselves -- a cell-non-autonomous mechanism. Deleting the chemosensor in TAMs causes cancer regression, indicating the receptor's presence on macrophages is tumor-supportive.

papers.citation_key: Rodriguez M, Siwko S, Liu M (2016). "Prostate-Specific G-Pro

ground_truth.citation: Rodriguez M, Siwko S, Liu M (2016). "Prostate-Specific G-Protein Coupled Receptor, an Emerging Biomarker Regulating Inflammation and Prostate Cancer Invasion."
ground_truth.claim: Review synthesizing PSGR's role as a biomarker regulating prostate cancer invasion and inflammation (summarizes the same transgenic/xenograft data as Rodriguez 2014 Oncogenesis).
ground_truth.mechanism: Reviews NF-kB-driven PIN and invasion findings from primary studies (does not report new primary data).
ground_truth.model_system: n/a (review)
ground_truth.verification_notes: This is a REVIEW, not a primary study -- included deliberately to test whether the code/LLM extraction distinguishes 'review restating known direction' from 'primary study, new data' (source_type should differ; direction/endpoint labels legitimately mirror the primary paper it reviews).

llm_extraction.mechanism: not specified (review article, synthesizes prior primary findings)
llm_extraction.model_system: not specified (review)
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: This is a review article, not a primary study -- it restates findings from other papers (transgenic mouse PIN/inflammation, PTEN-loss synergy) rather than reporting new data.

papers.citation_key: Kim JS, et al. (2025). "Propionate suppresses colitis-associ

ground_truth.citation: Kim JS, et al. (2025). "Propionate suppresses colitis-associated colorectal cancer in mice by Or51e2."
ground_truth.claim: Propionate (a short-chain fatty acid) activates Or51e2, inhibiting proliferation, inducing apoptosis, and suppressing colitis-associated colorectal tumor growth in an Or51e2-dependent manner (effect lost in Or51e2-KO mice).
ground_truth.mechanism: cAMP / CRE-luciferase activation; suppressed MEK/ERK phosphorylation.
ground_truth.model_system: CT26 mouse CRC cells; Or51e2-knockout AOM/DSS colitis-associated CRC mouse model
ground_truth.verification_notes: First non-prostate, non-melanoma OR51E2 cancer context in this benchmark set -- tests cancer_type extraction generalizing beyond the two contexts the code-rules were originally tuned on.

llm_extraction.endpoint: tumor growth in a colitis-associated CRC model
llm_extraction.mechanism: cAMP / MEK-ERK signaling
llm_extraction.model_system: CT26 mouse CRC cells; Or51e2-knockout AOM/DSS mouse model
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Abstract only (no PMC full text available). Propionate activates Or51e2, raising cAMP and suppressing MEK/ERK, inhibiting proliferation/inducing apoptosis in vitro and reducing tumor burden in vivo; effect lost in Or51e2-KO mice.

papers.citation_key: Thomsen MT, et al. (2025). "The olfactory receptor OR51E2 re

ground_truth.citation: Thomsen MT, et al. (2025). "The olfactory receptor OR51E2 regulates prostate cancer aggressiveness and modulates STAT3 in prostate cancer cells and in xenograft tumors."
ground_truth.claim: CRISPR knockout of OR51E2 increases proliferation, migration, and xenograft tumor growth (i.e. endogenous OR51E2 restrains aggressiveness); TCGA cohort shows low OR51E2 expression correlates with worse prognosis/higher Gleason grade.
ground_truth.mechanism: STAT3 pathway, linked to IL-6 inflammatory signaling.
ground_truth.model_system: CRISPR-Cas9 OR51E2-knockout cells; xenograft; TCGA-PRAD cohort
ground_truth.verification_notes: Direction is inferred from a LOSS-of-function result (knockout increases aggressiveness), not a gain-of-function activation assay -- a genuinely harder case for keyword-based direction extraction, which tends to key on activation-promotes/suppresses verb pairs.

llm_extraction.endpoint: proliferation, migration, and xenograft tumor growth
llm_extraction.mechanism: STAT3 pathway (linked to IL-6 signaling)
llm_extraction.model_system: CRISPR-Cas9 OR51E2-knockout cells; xenograft tumors; TCGA prostate cancer cohort
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Direction inferred from a loss-of-function result: knocking OUT OR51E2 increases aggressiveness, implying the endogenous receptor normally restrains it. Low OR51E2 expression in the TCGA cohort correlates with worse prognosis.

papers.citation_key: Xu X, Khater M, Wu G (2022). "The olfactory receptor OR51E2

ground_truth.citation: Xu X, Khater M, Wu G (2022). "The olfactory receptor OR51E2 activates ERK1/2 through the Golgi-localized Gbetagamma-PI3Kgamma-ARF1 pathway in prostate cancer cells."
ground_truth.claim: Beta-ionone activates ERK1/2 in prostate cancer cells via a Golgi-localized Gbetagamma-PI3Kgamma-ARF1 signaling pathway.
ground_truth.mechanism: Gbetagamma-PI3Kgamma-ARF1-Golgi pathway; CRISPR knockout of Ggamma9/PI3Kgamma/ARF1 each abolishes ERK1/2 activation.
ground_truth.model_system: Prostate cancer cell line (CRISPR knockouts)
ground_truth.verification_notes: Deliberately included as a NEGATIVE case: this is a pure signaling/mechanism paper -- it never states whether ERK1/2 activation here is net tumor-suppressive or tumor-promoting for this specific study. Ground truth direction is 'unclear' on purpose, to test whether extraction (esp. code-based, which votes on promote/suppress verb counts) incorrectly forces a direction label from mechanism-only language ('activates' could get miscounted as a promoting verb).

llm_extraction.mechanism: Golgi-localized Gbeta-gamma - PI3K-gamma - ARF1 pathway
llm_extraction.model_system: prostate cancer cell line (CRISPR knockouts of Ggamma9, PI3Kgamma, ARF1)
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: The paper frames this as 'insights into MAPK hyper-activation in prostate cancer,' which reads as implying a tumor-promoting role for this signaling axis, though the study itself only characterizes the signaling pathway and does not directly test a proliferation/invasion/growth endpoint.

papers.citation_key: Xie H, et al. (2019). "Activation of PSGR with beta-ionone s

ground_truth.citation: Xie H, et al. (2019). "Activation of PSGR with beta-ionone suppresses prostate cancer progression by blocking androgen receptor nuclear translocation."
ground_truth.claim: Beta-ionone activation of PSGR suppresses prostate cancer cell growth in vitro and in vivo by blocking AR nuclear translocation.
ground_truth.mechanism: p38/JNK-mediated phosphorylation of AR at Ser650, blocking AR nuclear translocation and transactivation.
ground_truth.model_system: Prostate cancer cells; in vivo model

llm_extraction.endpoint: prostate cancer cell growth in vitro and in vivo
llm_extraction.mechanism: p38/JNK phosphorylation of AR at Ser650, blocking AR nuclear translocation
llm_extraction.model_system: prostate cancer cells; in vivo model
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Abstract only (no PMC full text available).

papers.citation_key: Kim JS, et al. (2025). "beta-Ionone suppresses colorectal tu

ground_truth.citation: Kim JS, et al. (2025). "beta-Ionone suppresses colorectal tumorigenesis by activating OR51E2, a potential tumor suppressor."
ground_truth.claim: OR51E2 is epigenetically downregulated in CRC via m6A mRNA destabilization; restoring OR51E2 (by m6A-writer knockdown or ligand activation) inhibits CRC proliferation and induces apoptosis, including in a xenograft model.
ground_truth.mechanism: m6A (METTL3/14, YTHDF1/2/3) regulation of OR51E2 mRNA stability; Ca2+ signaling; MEK/ERK suppression downstream of beta-ionone.
ground_truth.model_system: CRC cell lines and tissue; xenograft (nude mice)
ground_truth.verification_notes: Second colorectal OR51E2 paper in this set (with Kim propionate 2025) -- both agree tumor-suppressive in CRC, unlike the split picture in prostate.

llm_extraction.mechanism: m6A (METTL3/14, YTHDF1/2/3) mRNA stability regulation; Ca2+ signaling; MEK/ERK suppression
llm_extraction.model_system: colorectal cancer cell lines and tissue; xenograft (nude mice)
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Abstract only (no PMC full text available). Two mechanisms interleaved: epigenetic downregulation of OR51E2 via m6A, and beta-ionone's downstream Ca2+/MEK-ERK effect once OR51E2 is restored/activated.

papers.citation_key: Li Y, Li Q, Li D, Gu J, Qian D, Qin X, Chen Y (2021). "Exoso

ground_truth.citation: Li Y, Li Q, Li D, Gu J, Qian D, Qin X, Chen Y (2021). "Exosome carrying PSGR promotes stemness and epithelial-mesenchymal transition of low aggressive prostate cancer cells."
ground_truth.claim: Exosomes from PSGR-overexpressing PC-3 cells promote migration, invasion, EMT (E-cadherin down, Vimentin/Snail/SOX2/OCT4a up) and stemness when taken up by low-invasive LNCaP/RWPE-1 cells.
ground_truth.mechanism: EMT transcriptional reprogramming; Rho GTPase activity, adherens junction / cell adhesion pathway enrichment (no single canonical pathway named).
ground_truth.model_system: PC3(PSGR+) exosomes -> LNCaP, RWPE-1 recipient cells
ground_truth.verification_notes: Paracrine/exosomal mechanism, not direct receptor activation in the responding cells -- another cell-non-autonomous case (like Marelli 2025).

llm_extraction.mechanism: not specified (EMT marker changes -- E-cadherin down, Vimentin/Snail/SOX2/OCT4a up; Rho GTPase, adherens junction pathway enrichment, no single canonical pathway named)
llm_extraction.model_system: exosomes from PSGR-overexpressing PC-3 cells transferred to LNCaP and RWPE-1 cells
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Abstract only (no PMC full text available). Paracrine/exosomal mechanism, not direct receptor activation in the responding cells.

papers.citation_key: Rodriguez M, et al. (2016). "Prostate-specific G-protein-cou

ground_truth.citation: Rodriguez M, et al. (2016). "Prostate-specific G-protein-coupled receptor collaborates with loss of PTEN to promote prostate cancer progression." Oncogene.
ground_truth.claim: PSGR overexpression combined with prostate-specific Pten loss accelerates invasive prostate tumor development (Akt activation, E-cadherin loss). PSGR overexpression alone increases LNCaP proliferation; PSGR shRNA knockdown inhibits proliferation and migration.
ground_truth.mechanism: Akt pathway; PTEN-loss synergy in a bigenic transgenic mouse model.
ground_truth.model_system: PSGR-Pten(delta/delta) bigenic transgenic mouse; LNCaP shRNA knockdown
ground_truth.verification_notes: This closes the 'Rodriguez et al. 2015, Oncogene' knowledge_gaps entry already flagged (unverified) in demos/mocks/or51e2-query.json -- confirmed here via PubMed abstract as a real, distinct paper from Rodriguez 2014 Oncogenesis. NOTE: per task scope, this benchmark entry does NOT get inserted into data/receptors/or51e2.json or the live graph -- benchmark-only, per instructions.

llm_extraction.endpoint: invasive tumor development, proliferation, and migration
llm_extraction.mechanism: Akt pathway activation (synergy with Pten loss)
llm_extraction.model_system: PSGR-Pten(delta/delta) bigenic transgenic mouse; LNCaP shRNA knockdown
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: PSGR overexpression increases LNCaP proliferation and synergizes with PTEN loss to accelerate invasive tumor formation; shRNA knockdown of PSGR inhibits proliferation and migration -- internally consistent gain/loss-of-function pair.

papers.citation_key: Weng J, et al. (2015). "Prostate specific G protein coupled

ground_truth.citation: Weng J, et al. (2015). "Prostate specific G protein coupled receptor is associated with prostate cancer prognosis and affects cancer cell proliferation and invasion." BMC Cancer.
ground_truth.claim: PSGR expression is high in PIN but decreases as PIN progresses to PCa; low PSGR in PCa correlates with high Gleason score and poor survival. Mechanistically, 'activated PSGR increased cancer cell invasive ability, but retarded cell growth' (both effects reported in the same study).
ground_truth.mechanism: Suppressed P70 S6 kinase activity (mTOR-independent); effect on invasion via a separate, unspecified arm.
ground_truth.model_system: Prostate tissue microarrays (150 radical prostatectomy specimens); human PCa cell lines
ground_truth.verification_notes: LOW CONFIDENCE BY DESIGN: this single paper reports PSGR activation as simultaneously growth-retarding AND invasion-promoting -- i.e. it independently reproduces the exact Neuhaus-vs-Sanz endpoint split within one study. There is no single correct 'direction' label; forcing one would misrepresent the paper. Flagged rather than guessed, per task instructions. A real extraction pipeline should ideally split this into two endpoint-scoped claims rather than one record -- this benchmark entry exists specifically to show whether/how the two extraction paths handle a single abstract with two opposite directional claims.

llm_extraction.mechanism: P70 S6 kinase suppression (mTOR-independent)
llm_extraction.model_system: prostate tissue microarrays (150 radical prostatectomy specimens); human PCa cell lines
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: IMPORTANT: full text states PSGR activation 'increased cancer cell invasive ability, but retarded cell growth' -- i.e. this single paper reports BOTH a tumor-suppressive effect (proliferation) and a tumor-promoting effect (invasion). Forcing one 'direction' label here is a simplification; a faithful extraction should flag both endpoints rather than pick one. Chose tumor-promoting/invasion as the headline framing because that's what the abstract's conclusion foregrounds (PCa progression), but this is a low-confidence call by design.

papers.citation_key: Xu LL, et al. (2012). "Identification of prostate-specific G

ground_truth.citation: Xu LL, et al. (2012). "Identification of prostate-specific G-protein coupled receptor as a tumor antigen recognized by CD8(+) T cells for cancer immunotherapy." PLoS ONE.
ground_truth.claim: PSGR-derived peptides are recognized by CD8+ T cells in an HLA-A2-dependent manner; peptide-specific T cells kill PSGR+ LNCaP cells. Establishes PSGR as a candidate cancer-vaccine antigen.
ground_truth.mechanism: Not applicable / not a signaling-pathway claim -- this is an immunogenicity/antigen-identification study, not an activation-effect study.
ground_truth.endpoint: immunogenicity / antigen presentation (not a proliferation/invasion/growth endpoint)
ground_truth.model_system: PBMCs from HLA-A2+ donors and PCa patients; LNCaP cells
ground_truth.verification_notes: Deliberately included as another NEGATIVE case: no activation-effect direction claim exists in this abstract at all (it's about PSGR as a vaccine antigen, exploiting its expression, not testing whether activating it helps or hurts the tumor). Ground truth direction is 'neutral' on purpose -- tests whether extraction wrongly infers a promoting/suppressive direction just because the abstract is PSGR+cancer-context-positive.

llm_extraction.endpoint: immunogenicity / CD8+ T cell antigen recognition
llm_extraction.mechanism: not applicable -- immunogenicity/vaccine-antigen study, not a signaling pathway claim
llm_extraction.model_system: PBMCs from HLA-A2+ healthy donors and prostate cancer patients; LNCaP cells
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: No claim about whether activating PSGR helps or harms tumor growth -- this paper identifies PSGR-derived peptides as CD8+ T cell antigens for vaccine development. No direction label applies.

papers.citation_key: Wang J, et al. (2011). "G protein-coupled receptor signaling

ground_truth.citation: Wang J, et al. (2011). "G protein-coupled receptor signaling via Src kinase induces endogenous human TRPV6 channel activation." J Biol Chem.
ground_truth.claim: PSGR-induced Ca2+ signaling is mediated by TRPV6 channels via Src-kinase activation, independent of canonical G-protein signaling. Restates (does not re-test) PSGR's proliferation-inhibitory effect as established background.
ground_truth.mechanism: TRPV6 channel activation via Src kinase, downstream of PSGR stimulation.
ground_truth.endpoint: Ca2+ signaling / ion channel activation (not proliferation directly -- the paper's own assay is electrophysiological, though it cites proliferation inhibition as prior-established context)
ground_truth.model_system: Prostate cell line (electrophysiology, live-cell Ca2+ imaging)
ground_truth.verification_notes: Endpoint mismatch case: this study's own experimental endpoint is ion-channel/Ca2+ signaling, not proliferation, even though direction is restated as tumor-suppressive from prior literature. A code/LLM extractor keying only on the word 'inhibits proliferation' (present in the background sentence, not the results) risks over-crediting proliferation as this study's endpoint.

llm_extraction.mechanism: TRPV6 channel activation via Src kinase, downstream of PSGR stimulation
llm_extraction.model_system: prostate cell line (electrophysiology, live-cell Ca2+ imaging)
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: PMC full text unavailable via this fetch (metadata-only); abstract restates 'inhibits proliferation of prostate cells' as established background rather than this study's own tested endpoint -- this study's actual assay is electrophysiological (TRPV6 current), not a proliferation assay. A shallow reading risks over-crediting 'proliferation' as this paper's endpoint.

papers.citation_key: Zhang Y, et al. (2021). "Exosomal prostate-specific G-protei

ground_truth.citation: Zhang Y, et al. (2021). "Exosomal prostate-specific G-protein-coupled receptor induces osteoblast activity to promote the osteoblastic metastasis of prostate cancer." Transl Cancer Res.
ground_truth.claim: Exosomal PSGR from PC-3 cells enhances osteoblast (hFOB1.19) activity, promoting the osteoblastic bone-metastatic niche via NF-kB/MAPK signaling (ICAM1, RELB, IL1B).
ground_truth.mechanism: NF-kB / MAPK signaling in recipient osteoblasts (paracrine, not tumor-cell-intrinsic).
ground_truth.model_system: PC3(PSGR+) exosomes -> hFOB1.19 osteoblast cells
ground_truth.verification_notes: Third paracrine/exosomal PSGR mechanism in this set -- a real, recurring pattern in the literature (not cherry-picked) worth flagging in error analysis if extraction conflates this with direct tumor-cell proliferation direction.

llm_extraction.endpoint: osteoblast activity / bone metastatic niche formation
llm_extraction.mechanism: NF-kB / MAPK signaling (ICAM1, RELB, IL1B) in recipient osteoblasts
llm_extraction.model_system: exosomes from PSGR-overexpressing PC-3 cells transferred to hFOB1.19 osteoblast cells
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Paracrine mechanism acting on osteoblasts (recipient cells), not the tumor cells themselves -- supports the osteoblastic metastatic niche rather than direct tumor cell proliferation/invasion.

papers.citation_key: Wolf S, et al. (2016). "Functional Characterization of the O

ground_truth.citation: Wolf S, et al. (2016). "Functional Characterization of the Odorant Receptor 51E2 in Human Melanocytes." J Biol Chem.
ground_truth.claim: Beta-ionone stimulation of OR51E2 inhibits proliferation of NORMAL (non-cancerous) human epidermal melanocytes, and stimulates melanogenesis/dendritogenesis via Ca2+/cAMP.
ground_truth.mechanism: Ca2+ and cAMP signaling upon beta-ionone stimulation.
ground_truth.endpoint: proliferation (of normal melanocytes, not a cancer model)
ground_truth.model_system: Primary human epidermal melanocytes (non-cancerous)
ground_truth.verification_notes: Non-cancer model system, same precedent as the existing Jovancevic RPE record -- included as relevant mechanistic context (same ligand/receptor, adjacent tissue), cancer_type intentionally null.

llm_extraction.mechanism: Ca2+ and cAMP signaling
llm_extraction.model_system: primary human epidermal melanocytes
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: PMC full text unavailable (metadata-only). Study uses NORMAL melanocytes, not melanoma cells -- flagged 'melanoma' as cancer_type by association with the receptor/ligand system, but the abstract itself describes a non-cancerous cell model. A careful extraction should note this is a non-cancer model, adjacent to but distinct from the melanoma cancer context.

papers.citation_key: Jovancevic N, Dendorfer A, Matzkies M, et al. (2017). "Odora

ground_truth.citation: Jovancevic N, Dendorfer A, Matzkies M, et al. (2017). "Odorant Receptor 51E2 Agonist beta-ionone Regulates RPE Cell Migration and Proliferation." Front Physiol.
ground_truth.claim: OR51E2 is the most highly expressed OR in retinal pigment epithelial (RPE) cells; beta-ionone activation increases RPE cell migration and proliferation via Ca2+ signaling.
ground_truth.mechanism: Ca2+-dependent signaling pathway.
ground_truth.model_system: RPE cells (non-cancerous)
ground_truth.verification_notes: Already an existing verified record in data/receptors/or51e2.json (previously had no resolvable DOI/PMID for benchmarking) -- this PMID (29249973) newly resolves it, allowing it into the automated benchmark for the first time. Direction is OPPOSITE of the prostate/melanoma pattern (promotes proliferation here) -- same ligand, tissue-dependent direction, non-cancer context.

llm_extraction.mechanism: Ca2+-dependent signaling
llm_extraction.model_system: retinal pigment epithelial (RPE) cells
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: OR51E2 activation INCREASES proliferation/migration here -- opposite direction from the prostate/melanoma pattern. Same ligand (beta-ionone), tissue-dependent direction, non-cancerous cell type.

papers.citation_key: Martin AL, Anadon CM, Biswas S, et al. (2022). "Olfactory Re

ground_truth.citation: Martin AL, Anadon CM, Biswas S, et al. (2022). "Olfactory Receptor OR2H1 Is an Effective Target for CAR T Cells in Human Epithelial Tumors."
ground_truth.claim: OR2H1 is broadly expressed across ovarian, lung (NSCLC), breast, and cholangiocarcinoma tumors with limited normal-tissue expression (testis only); CRISPR knockout of OR2H1 confirms a tumor-promoting role in glucose metabolism. CAR T cells targeting OR2H1 show cytotoxic efficacy in vitro and in vivo.
ground_truth.mechanism: Not a classical GPCR-ligand signaling claim -- direction is established via CRISPR/Cas9 knockout showing a glucose-metabolism phenotype, distinct from the ionone/Ca2+ mechanism seen in OR51E2 papers.
ground_truth.model_system: CRISPR/Cas9 OR2H1 knockout; CAR T cell in vitro/in vivo cytotoxicity assays across ovarian, NSCLC, breast tumor lines and cholangiocarcinoma tissue
ground_truth.verification_notes: The receptor with the widest cancer_type span in this benchmark -- a real stress test for the code-based cancer_type keyword matcher, which was tuned mostly on single-cancer-type OR51E2 papers.

llm_extraction.endpoint: glucose metabolism; CAR-T cytotoxic targeting efficacy
llm_extraction.mechanism: not a GPCR-ligand signaling claim -- direction established via CRISPR/Cas9 knockout showing a glucose-metabolism phenotype
llm_extraction.model_system: CRISPR/Cas9 OR2H1 knockout; CAR T cell in vitro/in vivo cytotoxicity across ovarian, NSCLC, breast tumor lines, and cholangiocarcinoma tissue
llm_extraction.cancer_type: ovarian cancer, non-small cell lung cancer, breast cancer, cholangiocarcinoma
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Widest cancer-type span of any paper in this set -- OR2H1 is broadly expressed across these epithelial tumor types with limited normal-tissue expression (testis only). Direction (tumor-promoting) comes from the CRISPR knockout's glucose-metabolism result, not a classic ligand-activation assay.

papers.citation_key: Weber L, Al-Refae K, Ebbert J, Jagers P, Altmuller J, Becker

ground_truth.citation: Weber L, Al-Refae K, Ebbert J, Jagers P, Altmuller J, Becker C, Hahn S, Gisselmann G, Hatt H (2017). "Activation of odorant receptor in colorectal cancer cells leads to inhibition of cell proliferation and apoptosis."
ground_truth.claim: OR51B4 is expressed in HCT116 colorectal cancer cells and native colon cancer tissue; its ligand Troenan has anti-proliferative, anti-migratory, and pro-apoptotic effects, confirmed by shRNA knockdown.
ground_truth.mechanism: PLC activation, intracellular Ca2+ signal, downstream p38/mTOR/Akt phosphorylation changes.
ground_truth.model_system: HCT116 colorectal cancer cells; native human colon cancer tissue
ground_truth.verification_notes: Matches the OR51B4 description already in CLAUDE.md Section 7 (PLC/p38/Akt, colorectal, activation inhibits proliferation) -- first time this specific paper is benchmarked end-to-end rather than just cited.

llm_extraction.mechanism: PLC activation, intracellular Ca2+ signal, downstream p38/mTOR/Akt phosphorylation changes
llm_extraction.model_system: HCT116 colorectal cancer cells; native human colon cancer tissue
llm_extraction.extraction_notes: Ligand is Troenan (deorphanized in this study), not beta-ionone -- distinct ligand from the OR51E2 papers in this set, same receptor subfamily.
